title: Trade Liberalization and Gender Inequality
abstract: We consider a model where firms differ in their productivity and workers are differentiated by skill and gender. A reduction in tariffs induces more productive firms to modernize their technology and enter the export market. New technologies involve computerized production processes and lower the need for physically demanding skills. As a result, the relative wage and employment of women improves in blue-collar tasks, but not in white-collar tasks. We empirically confirm these theoretical predictions using a panel of Mexican establishments and the tariff reductions associated with the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA).
summary_en: When a country cuts its import tariffs, who benefits — men or women? This paper builds and tests a theory where tariff cuts push productive firms to modernise their technology, which reduces the demand for physically demanding skills. The prediction: women's relative wages and employment rise in blue-collar jobs (where physical strength mattered most) but not in white-collar jobs. The authors confirm this using Mexican manufacturing data and NAFTA tariff cuts. Trade liberalisation can narrow gender gaps, but only on the factory floor, not in offices.
data_used: Mexican manufacturing establishment panel 1990–2000 from INEGI's Annual Industrial Survey; NAFTA (1994) tariff reductions at the industry level serve as the source of variation; ~6,000 establishments.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian gender-equity and trade-policy discussions: trade openness can reduce gender gaps in blue-collar sectors through technology upgrading, a channel rarely considered in policy discussions. Relevant as input to Ministry of Economy and Ministry of Families analysis of how EU trade agreements (e.g., Mercosur) interact with gender-outcome goals. Evidence is from Mexican tariff cuts; the mechanism generalises to any substantial trade-opening shock in a developing or middle-income economy.
